sections.title: Overview
sections.content: Shoulder pain may arise from rotator cuff-related pain, instability, acromioclavicular injury, stiffness, or referred pathology. Physio care should focus on red flags, function, and loading tolerance rather than imaging alone.

sections.title: Clinical Presentation
sections.content:
- Pain with reaching, lifting, overhead activity, or sleeping on the shoulder
- Weakness, apprehension, loss of range, or painful arc
- Possible traumatic onset after fall, tackle, lift, or dislocation
- Clicking, giving way, or inability to elevate the arm after injury

sections.title: Considerations
sections.content:
- Escalate for suspected fracture, unreduced dislocation, acute massive cuff tear, septic joint, neurovascular compromise, or referred cardiac/abdominal pain
- Imaging is most useful when trauma, dislocation, major weakness, or red flags are present
- Acute pain relief should be paired with progressive loading rather than repeated full rest

sections.title: Management - Primary
sections.content:
- Screen trauma mechanism, neurovascular status, and whether the patient can use the limb safely
- Support the arm, position for comfort, and advise relative unloading
- Escalate early if the patient cannot actively elevate after trauma, has deformity, or has systemic symptoms

sections.title: Management - Advanced
sections.content:
- Complete focused shoulder assessment including active range, resisted testing, instability screening, and cervical referral screen where appropriate
- Begin staged advice on activity modification, pendular or assisted movement, scapular control, and progressive reloading as tolerated
- Distinguish likely cuff-related pain, stiffness, instability, and AC joint injury to guide review timing
- Refer for imaging, injection discussion, or orthopaedic review when weakness, traumatic instability, or poor progress persists

sections.title: Additional Notes
sections.content: - Document function, sleep impact, work demands, and whether the pattern appears traumatic, atraumatic, or referred
